title: Rigidity phenomena in random point sets and applications
speaker: Subhroshekhar Ghosh
abstract: In several naturally occurring (infinite) point processes, we show that the number (and other statistical properties) of the points inside a finite domain are determined, almost surely, by the point configuration outside the domain. This curious phenomenon we refer to as "rigidity". We will discuss rigidity phenomena in point processes, and their applications to stochastic geometry and to random instances of some classical questions in Fourier analysis.

proof_stages.label: Model point processes and outside-inside question
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Poisson, Ginibre, and Gaussian analytic zero point processes, defines the two main correlated models, and formulates the problem of what the outside configuration of a bounded domain determines about the inside points.

proof_stages.label: Ginibre number rigidity and full tolerance
proof_stages.summary: For the Ginibre ensemble, the outside points determine exactly the number of inside points, but after conditioning on that number the inside configuration has an almost-everywhere positive density, with the same quadratic repulsion form as the original ensemble.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian-zero number-and-sum rigidity and full tolerance
proof_stages.summary: For zeros of the Gaussian analytic function, the outside configuration determines both the number and the sum of the inside zeros, and on the corresponding affine constraint manifold the conditional law is absolutely continuous with positive density.

proof_stages.label: Linear-statistic recovery mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The rigidity proof is sketched via scaled smooth linear statistics whose variance drops to zero, with a direct argument for Gaussian zeros and a multi-scale averaging device for the Ginibre ensemble.

proof_stages.label: Continuum percolation via tolerance
proof_stages.summary: The rigidity/tolerance theorems are applied to continuum percolation for Ginibre and Gaussian-zero processes, replacing Poisson spatial independence to prove phase transition and uniqueness of the infinite cluster.

proof_stages.label: Reconstruction and completeness applications
proof_stages.summary: The same rigidity ideas yield recovery of the Gaussian analytic function from its zeros up to an independent scalar and prove completeness of random reproducing-kernel or exponential systems for rigid determinantal processes.

proof_stages.label: Moment hierarchy and determinantal rigidity criteria
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by organizing point processes by how many power-sum moments the outside determines and by proving that a determinantal process with a proper non-projection contraction kernel is insertion/deletion tolerant, so rigidity can occur only in projection cases.

examples.name: Disk hole-probability comparison
examples.note: The hole probability for a disk is used only as a familiar statistic showing that Ginibre and Gaussian-zero processes can look similar despite later rigidity differences.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines disk hole probabilities and states the Ginibre and Gaussian-zero asymptotic orders agree without deriving them

passes.reason: A hole event is the zero-count/minimal configuration in a rotationally symmetric disk, used only as a comparison statistic.
the hole event is the empty minimum-count configuration in a round disk and is used only to compare asymptotics
the centered disk is maximally symmetric and the hole event is the empty zero-count lower-tail case, used only as a comparison statistic
The hole event is the empty/minimal configuration in a round disk, used only as a comparison statistic.
A round-disk hole event is the empty/minimal point configuration in a maximally symmetric domain, used only as a comparison statistic.
The hole event is the empty, minimum-count configuration in a round disk, used only as a comparison statistic.
The zero-point event in a round disk is an empty minimum-count symmetric event, and its matching asymptotics obstruct using hole probabilities to distinguish the two processes.
The centered disk hole is an empty/minimal-count event in a highly symmetric domain, used only as a comparison statistic.
a hole event is the empty/minimal-count configuration in a round, maximally symmetric disk, used only as a comparison statistic
The empty-in-a-disc hole event is rotationally symmetric and a zero-count extreme, and it shows hole asymptotics alone fail to distinguish Ginibre from GAF zeros.
a disk hole is a rotationally symmetric empty/minimum-count event used only as a comparison statistic
the hole event is taken in a round disk, a rotationally symmetric domain, only to compare asymptotics

examples.name: Finite fixed-size ensembles converging to Poisson
examples.note: The speaker uses finite rigid point processes, in particular n uniform points in a disk of area n whose limit is Poisson, to illustrate that rigidity need not pass to weak limits.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the example of n uniform points in a disk of area n and takes the weak limit to Poisson to show rigidity need not pass to limits

passes.reason: The fixed-size finite ensemble has a deterministic total count giving trivial rigidity, and its Poisson limit shows rigidity need not pass to limits.
the fixed-cardinality uniform ensembles in round disks have trivial deterministic total size and their Poisson limit disproves preservation of rigidity under weak limits
the fixed total number of points makes the finite ensembles trivially rigid while their Poisson limit is not, refuting limit preservation of rigidity
The fixed total count collapses number randomness in a uniform disk ensemble and its Poisson limit shows rigidity need not pass to limits.
Uniform fixed-size binomial ensembles in growing disks are symmetric finite rigid models used to show weak limits need not preserve rigidity.
The fixed-size binomial ensembles have trivial deterministic total count but converge to non-rigid Poisson, disproving rigidity under limits.
Fixed-size finite ensembles have only trivial finite-count rigidity, and their Poisson weak limit shows rigidity need not pass to limits.
The fixed-n uniform ensembles have trivial finite fixed-cardinality rigidity and show rigidity need not pass to weak limits.
fixed-size binomial ensembles in round windows have deterministic total count and show rigidity need not survive weak limits
The rotationally symmetric binomial n-point process in a growing disc is used to show finite fixed-size rigidity need not survive weak limits.
fixed finite cardinality gives a trivial zero-variance rigidity case used to show rigidity need not pass to weak limits
fixed finite total count gives only trivial rigidity, and its Poisson limit shows rigidity need not pass to limits

examples.name: Poisson Burton-Keane two-cluster surgery
examples.note: The Poisson proof of uniqueness by resampling many points inside a large disk visibly motivates the replacement of finite energy by conditional tolerance in the correlated processes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the Burton-Keane surgery by fixing outside points, adding many inside points, joining two infinite clusters, and deriving the contradiction

examples.name: Random phase multiplier obstruction
examples.note: Multiplying the Gaussian entire function by an independent uniform phase has the same law and zeros, forcing the reconstruction statement to be only up to an independent scalar.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks that multiplying the Gaussian entire function by an independent uniform phase preserves its law and zeros, explaining the obstruction to recovery
